sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A sleep lab studied memory consolidation after participants learned a set of spatial locations. One group had normal sleep, a second had selective slow-wave sleep disruption, and a third had selective REM disruption. The next morning, spatial recall scores out of 30 averaged 24, 16, and 22. Emotional-picture recognition scores out of 30 averaged 21, 20, and 15. Researchers concluded that different sleep stages may contribute differently to types of memory.
questions.stem: Which group had the lowest spatial recall score?

choices.D: All groups had identical spatial recall

explanations.B: Correct. The slow-wave disruption group averaged 16 on spatial recall, lower than normal sleep at 24 and REM disruption at 22. The pattern suggests slow-wave sleep was especially important for the spatial-location task.
explanations.A: The normal sleep group had the highest spatial recall score.
explanations.C: The REM disruption group scored lower than normal but higher than the slow-wave disruption group.
explanations.D: The spatial scores were 24, 16, and 22, so they were not identical.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Spatial recall and emotional-picture recognition after normal sleep or selective sleep-stage disruption.

questions.stem: Which task was most impaired by REM disruption?

choices.B: Spatial recall more than emotional recognition
choices.C: Both tasks improved after REM disruption

explanations.A: Correct. The REM disruption group scored 15 on emotional-picture recognition, well below normal sleep at 21 and slow-wave disruption at 20. Spatial recall was only modestly reduced in the REM-disrupted group.
explanations.B: Spatial recall was much more impaired by slow-wave disruption than by REM disruption.
explanations.C: Scores did not improve after REM disruption; emotional recognition fell sharply.
explanations.D: The reported scores vary across groups, so change is present.

questions.stem: What broader conclusion is supported by the pattern?

choices.C: Sleep stages may support different memory processes
choices.A: All sleep stages have identical cognitive functions
choices.B: Sleep affects only physical growth and never memory
choices.D: REM sleep is the only stage that matters for every task

explanations.C: Correct. The two memory outcomes show different vulnerabilities: spatial recall drops most after slow-wave disruption, while emotional recognition drops most after REM disruption. That supports a differentiated view of sleep and memory.
explanations.A: The results show stage-specific differences, not identical functions.
explanations.B: The passage directly measures memory effects of sleep disruption.
explanations.D: REM mattered for emotional recognition, but slow-wave disruption mattered more for spatial recall.

questions.stem: A student applying this research should most reasonably conclude what?

choices.D: Protecting sleep may help memory, but different tasks may depend on different sleep processes
choices.A: Skipping sleep improves both spatial and emotional memory
choices.B: Only emotional material should ever be studied before sleep
choices.C: Sleep stage findings mean studying is unnecessary

explanations.D: Correct. The normal-sleep group performed best overall, and specific disruptions harmed specific outcomes. A practical conclusion is that sleep supports learning, though the exact stage-memory relationship can vary by task.
explanations.A: The data show worse performance after sleep disruption, not improvement.
explanations.B: Spatial material also depended on sleep, especially slow-wave sleep.
explanations.C: Sleep may support consolidation, but encoding through studying is still necessary.

questions.stem: What is one important limitation of selective-disruption studies?

choices.C: Disrupting one stage may also fragment sleep or alter next-day alertness
choices.B: They prove every dream contains spatial information

explanations.C: Correct. Even targeted disruption can change overall sleep quality or next-day fatigue, so researchers must consider whether memory differences reflect stage-specific effects or broader sleep fragmentation.
explanations.A: The passage reports memory scores directly.
explanations.B: Dream content is not measured or inferred from the spatial task.
explanations.D: Sleep-stage research is directly relevant to consciousness and cognition.

questions.passage: An attention study asked participants to complete a sustained-response task for 20 minutes. At random times, the computer asked whether they were on task, mind-wandering, or deliberately planning. Participants reported mind-wandering on 32% of probes and deliberate planning on 14%. Reaction-time variability was highest around mind-wandering probes. Participants also made more commission errors during periods they later identified as mind-wandering. The researchers argued that consciousness can drift away from task goals even when eyes remain on the screen.
questions.stem: Which state was associated with the most unstable task performance?

choices.D: The passage does not identify a state

explanations.B: Correct. The passage states that reaction-time variability was highest around mind-wandering probes and that commission errors increased during those periods. Those are direct indicators of unstable sustained attention.
explanations.A: Deliberate planning was measured, but it was not identified as producing the highest variability.
explanations.C: Being fully on task would be expected to produce more stable performance, not the highest errors.
explanations.D: The passage explicitly identifies mind-wandering as the problematic state.

questions.stem: Commission errors during mind-wandering most likely reflect what?

choices.A: Reduced executive control over the task response
choices.D: A stable increase in crystallized intelligence

explanations.A: Correct. Commission errors occur when participants respond when they should not. Their increase during mind-wandering suggests attention has drifted from task rules and executive control over responses is reduced.
explanations.B: More errors are not evidence of perfect monitoring.
explanations.C: The passage does not show improved sensitivity; it shows poorer control.
explanations.D: Crystallized intelligence is unrelated to momentary response inhibition.

questions.stem: Why use random thought probes in this study?

choices.D: To sample participants' conscious state during the task rather than relying only on memory afterward
choices.A: To punish participants for slow responses

explanations.D: Correct. Random probes capture experience near the moment it occurs, reducing reliance on later reconstruction. This helps link reported conscious state to nearby task performance.
explanations.A: The probes ask about mental state; they are not punishments.
explanations.B: The study measures mind-wandering rather than eliminating it.
explanations.C: Visual acuity is not the outcome being sampled by thought probes.

questions.stem: A student having trouble reading dense passages might apply this finding by doing what?

choices.B: Use brief self-checks to notice when attention has drifted
choices.A: Assume staring at the page guarantees comprehension
choices.C: Eliminate all breaks regardless of fatigue

explanations.B: Correct. The study shows that eyes can remain on a task while consciousness drifts. Brief self-checks can help the student detect mind-wandering and return attention to the passage.
explanations.A: Visual fixation does not guarantee attention or comprehension.
explanations.C: Strategic breaks may help sustained attention; eliminating all breaks is not supported.
explanations.D: Reading faster would not necessarily restore executive control or comprehension.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by the study?

choices.A: Mind-wandering was associated with greater variability and more errors during sustained attention
choices.B: Mind-wandering is always harmful in every context
choices.C: Planning thoughts and mind-wandering are identical states
choices.D: The study proves consciousness never affects behavior

explanations.A: Correct. The results connect mind-wandering reports with specific task-performance costs. The conclusion should stay within sustained attention rather than claim mind-wandering is universally bad or behaviorally irrelevant.
explanations.B: Mind-wandering can sometimes support creativity or planning; this task showed costs in one context.
explanations.C: The study separated deliberate planning from mind-wandering.
explanations.D: The study shows conscious state was related to behavior, not unrelated.
